story:
Stanislaw and his son Daniel meet in the blue gloom of an abandoned cinema buried inside a vast depot on a space station. The cinema was the first built for leisure, but the station has grown so tall and dense that almost no one knows it remains. Above them, pallet carriers rumble. Getting there has been hard; the belts are made for ore, not people. Stan remembers a different time, when the mag-line carried everyone—fast, smooth, and retro—sliding past views of Falon, the planet below, pink clouds by day and spark-lit cities at night. As a boy he rode for hours, dodging guards, learning each curve and station. It connected everything and everyone.

Now the corporations own sectors and set boundaries for tariffs. The old places decay. Justice HQ sits on the bones of a first-wave all-comers church, its spire gone, its ceramographite walls—once engraved with figures of early landers and beasts—pressed into service beneath bland polymer sheets. The first prison was sculpted too, but the beauty downstairs is hidden by cladding. Stan mourns what is buried and ignored. Daniel has heard it all. He has a job lined up on the far side, where green spaces and new builds promise comfort. The coming project is huge: an eighteen-kilometre solar funnel to draw power for the station and beam most of it to Falon. It will bring work for generations. It will also steal the view from Sectors 9 to 12 and tear down Sector 13 for anchor cables as thick as a ten-lane highway.

Stan unfolds a hand-drawn station map. A blue lattice overlays the decks: the buried mag-line, still tracing old paths to pools, trash compressors, hospitals, this very cinema. The tunnels are collapsed or filled, but the electromagnetic couplings still run in an unbroken chain. These neglected nodes, he says, are like spots of decay in a growing tooth. If you line them up, they become a fault.

The plan is simple and terrible. Send a pulse from the old power hub into the couplings. The shock will rot the foundations of the first-wave structures—already weakened by neglect—without toppling towers outright. Nothing dramatic will happen until the sun funnel comes online. Then, as the new cables load the frame, the station will split along the line that corruption created. The separation will be slow, about a month long—enough time to evacuate. It must fall, Stan says. The station has become a floating scab, a machine for profit that consumes its people and history. Breaking it is the only way to stop the injustice and force a reckoning.

Daniel won’t do it. He doesn’t see a revolt, only ordinary life: holidays, a middle class, decent homes on the far side. He refuses to sacrifice lives for his father’s cause. And he has already chosen. A week earlier, under orders from Justice HQ, he searched Stan’s home, found this very diagram, and set the trap.

Shutters high on the cinema wall grind open, flooding the dust with cold light. Justice officers rush in. Stan doesn’t run. Daniel looks away. The system has served him well, and his family’s status will rise because of what he has done. He will visit his father, he tells himself. He hopes they place Stan in the prison's older, lower section—where the ceramographite bricks were carved long ago with the heroes of the first wave. Stan’s gaze flicks once to Daniel. “You’ll tell yourself you’ll visit me.”
